context.project: Archon — AI skills framework for Claude Code. Has 98 skills, 8 lifecycle hooks, 5 synapses, 2 existing MCP servers (skill-router, agent-router) using FastMCP 2.14.5. Latest commit: 160fbc4 (hooks architecture + adversarial review fixes). 8 commits unpushed on main. NOT pushed — awaiting approval.
context.existing-plan: A v2 planning document with 17 verified research findings covering: - 4 pain points (PP-1 through PP-4) with the built-in tools - FastMCP 2.14.5 verified working on Python 3.13 / Windows 11 - MCP tool naming convention: mcp__{server-name}__{tool-name} (no conflicts) - Official MCP filesystem server analyzed (still uses string matching — same problem) - Line-number editing, difflib, CRLF detection, atomic writes all verified - 6 planned tools: file_read, file_edit, file_write, file_insert, file_search, file_structure - 12 decided design decisions, 5 risks assessed READ THIS FILE FIRST — it contains all prior research.

problem.title: Edit tool: exact string matching is fragile

problems.problem: Claude's Edit tool requires old_string to match EXACTLY. Whitespace, CRLF vs LF, invisible chars, non-unique strings — all cause failures. This breaks multiple times per session, every session. It is the single biggest source of friction.

problem.title: Read tool: line numbers pollute content

problems.problem:
The Read tool returns content in "cat -n" format with line numbers baked into the text (e.g., " 42→ def hello():"). When Claude then tries to use this content in Edit's old_string, IT INCLUDES THE LINE NUMBERS, which obviously don't exist in the actual file. This causes Edit to fail constantly.
Even worse: Claude sometimes copies these line-numbered strings into Write operations, literally writing line numbers into the file content.
The line numbers are useful for navigation but POISONOUS for editing workflows.

problem.title: Write tool: full-file overwrite is dangerous

problems.problem: Write requires reproducing the entire file. Large files get truncated, sections fall out of context window, content drifts. For a 3-line change in a 500-line file, Claude must correctly regenerate all 500 lines.
Each Edit call is independent. 5 edits to one file = 5 sequential calls, each of which can fail. Line shifts cascade and break subsequent edits.

task.label: evaluate-rtk
task.instruction:
Thoroughly evaluate https://github.com/rtk-ai/rtk to determine if and how it can help solve the problems above. Research specifically:
1. RTK's `rtk read` command — how it handles file reading, filtering, and whether it solves the line-number pollution problem (P-2)
2. RTK's hook system — it uses PreToolUse hooks to rewrite Bash commands. Can it intercept Read/Edit/Write tools, or is it Bash-only?
3. Whether RTK has or plans any edit/write functionality
4. Token compression benefits vs. overhead costs (there's data on this)
5. Windows compatibility (we're on Windows 11)

known-findings.comment: Research we already did — verify but don't re-discover
known-findings.finding: "Claude Code native tools bypass RTK hook — majority of token-heavy ops never reach Bash." RTK only intercepts the Bash tool. Built-in Read, Grep, Glob, Edit, Write are INVISIBLE to RTK's hook. ~60% of token-heavy operations bypass RTK entirely.

"read before write rules are broken." When RTK rewrites Read to rtk read, Claude Code's internal "read before write" policy breaks because the native Read was never called — only rtk read via Bash. Edit then fails because it requires a prior native Read. Users report losing time and tokens on re-reads.

"RTK Hook Increases Claude Code Costs by 18%." On non-compressible content, RTK's format transformation costs MORE tokens than it saves. Real-world testing showed +11% costs on files with low compression potential. RTK maintainers acknowledged this is expected: "RTK is a compression proxy. When content has low entropy, there's no room to compress and the overhead works against you."
"rtk read does not support reading specific lines." Users who force Claude to use rtk read lose the ability to read line ranges, causing full file reads.

rtk read supports: FilterLevel (None/Minimal/Aggressive), language-aware comment stripping, smart_truncate, optional line numbers (--line-numbers flag, OFF by default), head/tail. Line numbers use "N │ content" format, only when explicitly requested. The default is raw content without line numbers.
RTK's AggressiveFilter: keeps function/struct/class signatures, strips function bodies. Uses regex FUNC_SIGNATURE pattern. Falls back to raw content if filter produces empty output.

evaluation-questions.question: Can RTK solve P-2 (line number pollution)? Its rtk read defaults to no line numbers — but RTK only works via Bash, and Claude Code prefers built-in Read. Would we need to force Claude to use bash cat/rtk read instead? What are the tradeoffs?

RTK is a CLI proxy (Rust binary). Our existing plan uses an MCP server (Python FastMCP). Are these complementary, competing, or orthogonal? Could we use RTK for Bash commands AND MCP for tool-level operations?
Issue #1005 shows RTK breaks the read-before-write policy. Does this mean RTK is fundamentally incompatible with the Edit tool workflow? Or is there a workaround?
RTK has 100+ command handlers but NO edit/write/MCP functionality. Is it worth installing for the git/test/build compression alone, while using our MCP server for file operations?
RTK has Windows binaries. Has anyone tested it with Claude Code on Windows? Any known issues?

task.label: decide-and-plan
task.instruction:
Based on the RTK evaluation, decide the path forward. The options are:
OPTION A: MCP server only (our original plan) Build the file-ops MCP server with line-number editing. Ignore RTK entirely. Our tools handle reads/edits/writes. RTK can't help with the core problems.
OPTION B: RTK + MCP hybrid Install RTK for what it's good at (git/test/build/lint compression). Build MCP server for file operations (read/edit/write/search/structure). They're complementary — RTK handles Bash output compression, MCP handles file editing.
OPTION C: RTK-enhanced approach If RTK has features or planned features that overlap with our MCP plan, consider leveraging them instead of building from scratch.
OPTION D: Something else entirely If your research reveals a better approach we haven't considered.
Update c:/Users/tahaa/omniskill/.claude/rw-tools-plan.xml with the decision and reasoning. Add a new section with your findings.

task.label: build-it
task.instruction:
After deciding, BUILD the solution. Don't just plan. Execute.
If building the MCP server: - Follow the implementation steps in rw-tools-plan.xml - Follow patterns from servers/skill-router/server.py exactly - Register in ~/.claude/settings.json - Test via mock JSON-RPC stdin
If installing RTK: - Check Windows binary availability - Install and configure - Test the hook integration - Document what works and what doesn't
If hybrid: - Do both, in the right order

constraints.rule: READ rw-tools-plan.xml FIRST — it has 17 verified findings from real research
Don't re-research what's already verified. Build on the existing research.
Don't declare something works without testing it on this Windows 11 system
Don't push to remote without explicit approval
The core problem to solve is P-1 (Edit string matching) and P-2 (line number pollution). Everything else is secondary.
RTK is ONLY relevant if it helps solve OUR specific problems. Token compression on git/test output is nice-to-have, not the goal.
